jokes.text: A bird flew into my mouth this morning.
jokes.setup: Ilana says she needs to zen out, implying stress or chaos in her life.
jokes.punchline: The reason is an utterly absurd and inexplicable one — a bird flew into her mouth — delivered with total casualness.
jokes.explanation: The throwaway delivery is what makes this land. Ilana's chaos-magnet life is perfectly encapsulated in one surreal image delivered as if it's a mild inconvenience. The 'again?' follow-up elevates it further, implying this is a recurring problem which deepens the absurdism.

jokes.text: Eww, again?
jokes.setup: Ilana says a bird flew into her mouth, already absurd.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's 'again?' reacts with disgust but also implies this has happened before, retroactively deepening the absurdism.
jokes.explanation: One word does enormous comedic work. 'Again?' is structurally perfect — it validates the absurdity as a pattern while simultaneously shocking the audience with the implication. The economy here is nearly flawless.

jokes.text: Well, what could be more Zen than yoga that's suggested donation... Free.
jokes.setup: Ilana wants to zen out. Abbi offers a solution.
jokes.punchline: The 'suggested donation' pause before clarifying 'Free' reveals that Abbi has thought about the framing of this pitch, and the free part is the actual draw.
jokes.explanation: Solid joke about broke NYC millennials dressing up cheapness as spiritual practice. The beat between 'suggested donation' and 'Free' is well-structured but not especially surprising.

jokes.text: You catch that, suggested donation. Free.
jokes.setup: Abbi already made the 'suggested donation / Free' joke.
jokes.punchline: She immediately congratulates herself by pointing it out with 'You catch that?' — a meta callback to her own wordplay, now becoming a running bit.
jokes.explanation: The self-congratulatory 'You catch that?' immediately sets up a running gag that pays off later ('that's the price you pay'). It works because it's self-aware without being too winking.

jokes.text: Ooh, so it really smells in here. / Okay, this isn't 'Real Housewives' yoga, NeNe.
jokes.setup: They've arrived at the free yoga class.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's complaint about the smell gets shut down by Abbi calling her 'NeNe' (as in NeNe Leakes, Real Housewives cast member), painting Ilana as a diva in a free yoga class.
jokes.explanation: The 'NeNe' reference is a sharp characterization tool — it nails the gap between Ilana's diva instincts and their actual budget. The Real Housewives namecheck gives it a specific texture that elevates it above a generic 'you're being bougie' joke.

jokes.text: Sure, it has a few flaws, but that's the price you pay. / You catch that, that's the price you pay.
jokes.setup: Ilana complains about the smell; Abbi defends the class.
jokes.punchline: Abbi again self-narrates her own wordplay ('that's the price you pay' about a free yoga class), payoff to the 'You catch that?' running bit.
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the running gag works well. 'The price you pay' about something that's free is a solid pun, and Abbi's self-congratulation now has established structure. The 'No, I... I get it' from Ilana is the perfect deflation.

jokes.text: Two of us to get in here. Doesn't seem like enough space for us... / It's plenty. / Is it? / Yep, now it is.
jokes.setup: They ask people to scootch so they can share a crowded yoga space.
jokes.punchline: The dialogue implies they are squeezing aggressively into a space that clearly isn't big enough, displacing other yogis with cheerful entitlement.
jokes.explanation: Functional physical comedy — the visual of them aggressively squeezing in against reluctant yogis while Abbi chirps 'Just enough' sells the joke. Slightly more dependent on performance/visuals than dialogue alone.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, you can go deeper. Okay. On both shoulders.
jokes.setup: Yoga class in progress; Ilana has positioned herself next to Abbi.
jokes.punchline: Ilana is coaching Abbi through yoga adjustments with the cadence and content of something more intimate/erotic — 'go deeper' and 'on both shoulders' delivered with total sincerity in a public class.
jokes.explanation: Mild double entendre that the show deploys casually. It's in keeping with Ilana's character and the show's general sensibility. Not particularly crafted but functionally funny.

jokes.text: Did you pack your anti-depressants? / Yep. / Your antianxiety? / Yep. / Please tell me antigas. / Yep. / Your vibrator? / Yep. / Okay, do you have your electric toothbrush?
jokes.setup: Abbi calls Ilana to check that she's packed all her essentials.
jokes.punchline: The list escalates from reasonable (antidepressants, antianxiety meds) to funny (antigas) to raunchy (vibrator), then back to mundane (electric toothbrush) — the return to mundane after 'vibrator' is the perfect punctuation.
jokes.explanation: The structural escalation here is very well done. The list comedy works because it builds logically from healthcare needs → bodily comfort → sexual wellness → dental hygiene, treating all of them with equal gravitas. The vibrator's placement is perfect — it's not the last item, which would be too easy. Returning to 'electric toothbrush' afterward normalizes it perfectly. Character-specific because only these two would have this exact checklist and conversation.

jokes.text: Great, we're both... totally packed responsibly.
jokes.setup: Abbi has just recited a packing list including a vibrator.
jokes.punchline: 'Responsibly' is the deliberately wrong word — the pause before it signals Abbi's own awareness that this is an absurd list to call responsible.
jokes.explanation: Clean button on the packing list sequence. The ironic 'responsibly' is a well-placed deadpan capper. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Pssht, always do.
jokes.setup: Abbi tells Ilana to 'wear something sexy' for their trip.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's immediate 'always do' — the presumption that she is always dressed sexy is delivered as if it's simply a factual baseline, not a boast.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Ilana. The confidence is the joke — no defensiveness, no irony, just the settled assumption that she is perpetually sexy. Very character-specific.

jokes.text: I have no time, fuck me, dude. Um, okay, my toothbrush, my vibrator. What'd she say, my meds?
jokes.setup: Abbi now has to pack herself in a rush. She narrates her own packing.
jokes.punchline: Her priorities mirror Ilana's list but in compressed, panicked form — and she's clearly forgotten which came first, 'meds' or 'vibrator,' prioritizing the vibrator automatically.
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the packing list — the vibrator now comes first in Abbi's frantic recollection, which is a character-specific and structurally satisfying inversion.

jokes.text: This is Lincoln's suitcase. / I just have... y'know, sometimes I feel like he broke up with me too.
jokes.setup: Ilana realizes the suitcase she's borrowing belonged to Lincoln, her ex.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's grief is that Lincoln broke up with her too — she processes the breakup as something that also happened to her, not just Abbi, which is both absurd and deeply character-true.
jokes.explanation: This is excellent character comedy. Ilana's possessiveness over Abbi's relationships — her emotional codependency — is the entire joke. 'He broke up with me too' is a perfectly written line that would only work for Ilana. There's also a genuine emotional undertone that makes it land harder.

jokes.text: God, are you flexing? / No. / Can you? / Okay.
jokes.setup: In the middle of packing and an emotional moment about Lincoln's suitcase.
jokes.punchline: Ilana breaks the emotional beat to ask if Abbi is flexing, then immediately asks her to flex on command — pure Ilana objectifying Abbi at the least appropriate moment.
jokes.explanation: The timing is what makes this. The emotional deflation followed by Ilana's completely unrelated physical appreciation of Abbi is a perfect tonal whiplash. The quick 'Okay' compliance from Abbi is the best possible button — she just goes along with it without question.

jokes.text: Uh, Abbi? Do we live in a zoo? Because I'm pretty sure there's an elephant in the room.
jokes.setup: Bevers has apparently noticed Abbi is using 'his' suitcase and wants to bring it up.
jokes.punchline: His chosen idiom is 'elephant in the room' but he sets it up with the bizarrely over-extended 'do we live in a zoo?' prefix, making him sound both dim and self-congratulatory about his own turn of phrase.
jokes.explanation: Classic Bevers — he uses an idiom correctly but with the setup of someone who thinks they've invented it. The 'do we live in a zoo?' pre-amble reveals exactly how his brain works. Good character-specific joke.

jokes.text: Drew Barrymore wrote an entire article about this suitcase. I splurged on this.
jokes.setup: Bevers claims the suitcase is his.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's defense is that Drew Barrymore endorsed it — a celebrity appeal to authority about luggage ownership — and she 'splurged,' implying this is a significant purchase for her.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of Drew Barrymore is funny — it's a very Abbi reference (aspirational, slightly dated, mid-tier celebrity). The Drew Barrymore bit seeds a callback that pays off with the flight attendant later, which elevates this joke in retrospect.

jokes.text: I thought Melodie bought me that suitcase. / I use it so much. / You do? / Just once.
jokes.setup: Bevers claims the suitcase, saying he thought it was a gift to him.
jokes.punchline: He claims to use it 'so much,' deflating immediately to 'just once' when challenged. His self-delusion collapse in real time is the gag.
jokes.explanation: The rapid self-correction from 'so much' to 'just once' is a perfect Bevers beat — he overclaims, gets challenged, immediately folds. The pacing is tight and the character logic is sound.

jokes.text: You guys are so Vivian Leigh and Laurence Olivier.
jokes.setup: Bevers asks about Abbi's romantic situation with Trey.
jokes.punchline: Comparing Abbi and Trey to Vivian Leigh and Laurence Olivier — two actors famous for a notoriously tumultuous, artistically intense and ultimately doomed relationship — is both weirdly specific and slightly wrong as a romantic compliment. Bevers has deployed this at maximum inappropriateness.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: Bevers using a wildly specific and slightly tragic cultural reference to describe a casual gym fling, and the fact that Vivian Leigh/Olivier were famously miserable together. Whether he knows that or not makes the joke ambiguous and funnier.

jokes.text: I have a way with words. / No, thank you, Bevers.
jokes.setup: Bevers offers to talk to Trey on Abbi's behalf, citing his gift for communication.
jokes.punchline: Given everything Bevers has just said — 'do we live in a zoo,' 'Vivian Leigh and Laurence Olivier' — his claim to have 'a way with words' is absurdly, demonstrably wrong. Abbi's flat 'No, thank you, Bevers' is the correct and perfectly timed response.
jokes.explanation: This is a mini-callback payoff. We've just watched Bevers's 'way with words' fail spectacularly twice, so his self-assessment lands as a perfect irony. Abbi's 'No, thank you, Bevers' is a flawless sitcom button — polite but absolute.

jokes.text: Well, if you're not gonna say it, I will. I'm gonna miss you too.
jokes.setup: Abbi is packing to leave on her trip and Bevers is hovering.
jokes.punchline: Bevers projects the sentiment onto himself — saying 'I'm gonna miss you too' while Abbi has said nothing of the sort — revealing his emotional neediness and obliviousness simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: 'Well, if you're not gonna say it, I will' is excellent misdirection — it implies he's about to give voice to something Abbi is suppressing, but what he says is the thing he wants to feel, not her. The structure of preempting someone else's emotion to express your own is a great character comedy beat.

jokes.text: please leave the radio on when you're gone for Rat Bastard and her family. / We can afford it.
jokes.setup: Bevers gives Abbi final instructions before she leaves.
jokes.punchline: 'Rat Bastard and her family' is apparently the name — or Bevers's name — for the rats in the apartment, treated with domestic tenderness. 'We can afford it' normalizes the absurdity completely.
jokes.explanation: Rat Bastard is a great joke on multiple levels: the affectionate naming of a rat, the implication that she has a family (a colony), and the earnest 'we can afford it' treating this as a shared domestic responsibility. Highly quotable and character-specific — Bevers treating rats as household members is perfect for his oblivious domesticity.

jokes.text: Just forgot my highlighters. / Nice wrist technique.
jokes.setup: Jaimé walks into a room where Abbi has clearly been masturbating with a vibrator.
jokes.punchline: The stranger compliments her 'wrist technique' on the way out — acknowledging what they saw without embarrassment, treating it as a technical skill.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the vibrator packing, the total non-reaction from Jaimé entering, and then the departing compliment on 'wrist technique' is perfectly economical shock comedy. The compliment reframes what was awkward as a skill to be admired, which is the Broad City sensibility at its best.

jokes.text: Hey! I saw that! Get back here! Shit! / I saw you! Shit. Fuck off! Fuck you! God dammit! Fuck!
jokes.setup: Abbi is rushing to catch the subway, presumably late and laden with luggage.
jokes.punchline: A rapid string of expletives as Abbi apparently chases someone or misses something in a full panic — the escalating profanity charting her emotional state is the entire joke.
jokes.explanation: More effective as visual comedy than text comedy. The string of expletives in a rush is energetic and character-true but not especially crafted in terms of dialogue. Functionally funny via performance.

jokes.text: You know, sometimes the flight is actually my favorite part. Hit up Hudson News, get some adult magazines. Looking at boobs is the best way to relax during turbulence. It's nature's Xanax.
jokes.setup: They're on the subway heading to the airport, excited about the trip.
jokes.punchline: 'Adult magazines' are framed as a legitimate relaxation strategy for turbulence, and then the coinage 'nature's Xanax' is delivered as if it's settled science. The reframe of boobs as anxiety medication is the punchline.
jokes.explanation: 'Nature's Xanax' is an excellent coinage — it has the compressed logic of a great joke, applying a clinical framework to something primal and absurd. Very Ilana in its total confidence and specificity. The 'best way to relax during turbulence' framing makes it a life hack, which amplifies the ridiculousness.

jokes.text: You know, sometimes if I forget the mags, I look down at my own.
jokes.setup: Ilana has just described adult magazines as her turbulence relaxation strategy.
jokes.punchline: She also uses her own breasts as a backup — treating self-directed appreciation of her own body as a perfectly reasonable contingency plan.
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke of the sequence and scores 8+ because it does several things simultaneously: it extends the absurdist logic of the prior joke, it's purely Ilana in its unselfconscious confidence and body positivity, and it subverts the setup one more time in an unexpected direction. 'I look down at my own' is the most economical possible phrasing. The beat of Abbi's shocked 'What?' followed by Ilana being baffled why she wouldn't is pitch-perfect dynamic comedy.

jokes.text: Oh, I... Okay, I was talking about adult magazines like 'Real Simple' or 'O' or 'Dwell.'
jokes.setup: Ilana assumed 'adult magazines' meant pornography; Abbi's 'what?' implied she was shocked.
jokes.punchline: Abbi reveals she was talking about aspirational lifestyle magazines all along — 'Real Simple,' 'O,' 'Dwell.' The reframe catches the audience and Ilana. Abbi's 'adult magazines' = magazines for adults.
jokes.explanation: This is excellent misdirection. The script leads us to assume alongside Ilana that 'adult magazines' means pornography. Abbi's correction — 'Real Simple,' 'O,' 'Dwell' — is both surprising and a perfect character revelation. These are very specifically Abbi magazines (aspirational, domestic, self-improvement). The character contrast between the two women is articulated purely through magazine taste. The structure is near-perfect: setup, apparent agreement, complete subversion.

jokes.text: I was talking about that, too.
jokes.setup: Abbi has just revealed she meant lifestyle magazines by 'adult magazines.'
jokes.punchline: Ilana instantly pivots to claim she also meant lifestyle magazines — either covering her embarrassment or genuinely reinventing her meaning on the fly. Delivered without a beat.
jokes.explanation: The button on the adult magazines sequence. Ilana's instant pivot is pure shamelessness — she refuses to be caught out and just claims the position. The no-beat delivery ('I was talking about that, too') is perfectly economical. It's funnier because we know she absolutely was not, and her confidence in the pivot is the joke.

jokes.text: A passenger at the next stop threw herself on the tracks. / Jesus, use a bridge. / Selfish.
jokes.setup: The subway is stopped because of a passenger on the tracks — a serious, tragic situation.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'Jesus, use a bridge' and Abbi's 'Selfish' are immediate, callous responses to a suicide attempt — treating a tragedy as a personal inconvenience.
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that walks a line. The instant callousness — the immediate pivot from 'tragedy' to 'inconvenience' — is played for the recognizable NYC commuter psychology where self-preservation instinct overrides compassion. 'Use a bridge' is blunt enough to shock; 'Selfish' from Abbi (the more 'sensible' character) is the better joke — it's funnier because it's Abbi, not Ilana, who goes there.

jokes.text: It's okay. You know what? I padded the travel time. We are all good, we're all good. It's not like we left our passports or anything.
jokes.setup: Abbi reassures Ilana that the subway delay is manageable because she planned ahead.
jokes.punchline: The specific reassurance 'It's not like we left our passports' is immediately followed by Abbi's horror as she realizes she has done exactly that.
jokes.explanation: Classic setup/punchline structure executed crisply. The specificity of Abbi's chosen reassurance — 'It's not like we left our passports' — is the joke. The audience can see the punchline coming from the moment she says it, but the payoff still lands because the structure is so clean. The timing between the line and the realization is perfectly comic.

jokes.text: Okay, it's been 20 minutes. How long does it take to move a fucking body off the tracks?
jokes.setup: They're still stuck on the subway because of the person on the tracks.
jokes.punchline: Ilana frames the delay as a logistical puzzle — 'How long does it take to move a fucking body?' — with complete obliviousness to the horror of the question.
jokes.explanation: An extension of the dark subway comedy. The clinical framing ('move a fucking body') is consistent with the show's willingness to go to uncomfortable places. Less surprising than the 'use a bridge' joke because we've already established this is how they're processing it.

jokes.text: Do you have any tape? / Do I have any tape? / Yeah, I have tape. / A bunch of different kinds. / Really cute.
jokes.setup: Abbi is desperately stressed about missing their flight.
jokes.punchline: In the middle of the crisis, Ilana has a wide variety of tapes in her bag — different kinds, presented with quiet pride. 'Really cute' is Abbi's resigned appreciation of Ilana's chaotic preparedness.
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy — the visual of Ilana pulling out an assortment of tapes (presumably various widths and colors) during a crisis while Abbi says 'Really cute' is a perfect snapshot of their dynamic. Ilana is inexplicably prepared for some things and completely unprepared for others.

jokes.text: Jesus, it stinks. / Bologna? / Ugh, it's unbelievable, right? / It is unbelievable. / So salty.
jokes.setup: They're stuck in a subway car in crisis mode.
jokes.punchline: A sudden olfactory discovery — Bologna — interrupts the crisis energy. The communal tasting/smelling of the Bologna odor, culminating in 'so salty,' is an absurdly mundane sidebar in the middle of their emergency.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from crisis to minutiae is the joke — their capacity to become fully absorbed in a Bologna smell while their flight ticks away is very Broad City. 'So salty' as a conclusion is a funny specific detail.

jokes.text: It's like 9/11 all over again. / It's 9/112! / Return of the 9/11s!
jokes.setup: Abbi is catastrophizing about missing their flight.
jokes.punchline: Comparing a missed flight to 9/11 is already transgressive; Ilana extends the absurdity to '9/112' and 'Return of the 9/11s,' treating terrorism as a sequel franchise.
jokes.explanation: The '9/112' and 'Return of the 9/11s' escalation is very funny because it treats 9/11 as a sequel franchise — applying blockbuster movie sequel logic to tragedy. The joke earns its darkness through structural cleverness rather than just shock value. Broad City specializes in this kind of NYC-specific irreverence.

jokes.text: You know, I once saw this documentary on Vimeo about these mole people who live down in the subway. I bet that they would help us. We would probably just have to give them our shoes and cut off a little bit of our hair, but... we'll get new shoes.
jokes.setup: Ilana presents an escape plan if the subway doesn't move in 30 minutes.
jokes.punchline: The mole people documentary (on Vimeo — not Netflix, Vimeo) has given Ilana very specific intelligence about subway people: you give them shoes and hair. 'We'll get new shoes' as the breezy conclusion is perfect.
jokes.explanation: This scores 8+ because the joke has layers: the Vimeo-specific documentary detail (not Netflix — Vimeo, which feels plausibly obscure and earnest), the complete belief in mole people lore, the specificity of the tribute (shoes and hair), and the breezy 'we'll get new shoes' that dismisses the sacrifice. It's absurdist worldbuilding delivered with total Ilana confidence. 'Truly impressed with you at every turn' from Abbi is the perfect button.

jokes.text: Truly impressed with you at every turn.
jokes.setup: Ilana has just proposed appeasing mole people with shoes and hair.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's sincere-sounding 'Truly impressed with you at every turn' is the perfect deadpan button — it could be read as genuine or sarcastic, and works both ways.
jokes.explanation: The perfect straight-man button on the mole people joke. Abbi's line works because the delivery can be completely sincere — she may actually be impressed, which is even funnier. Economy here is nearly perfect.

jokes.text: Let's say we were stuck in a subway forever... Who would we fuck? Who would we marry? Who would we eat?
jokes.setup: They've been stuck in the subway for 25 minutes.
jokes.punchline: Abbi proposes a game — but it's a modified 'fuck/marry/kill' where 'kill' has been replaced with 'eat,' consistent with the apocalyptic/mole people logic of the previous beat.
jokes.explanation: The substitution of 'eat' for 'kill' in the classic game is a clever subversion that fits perfectly within the apocalyptic framing they've just established. Abbi proposing this game (rather than Ilana) is a good character flip — Abbi going dark is funnier than Ilana doing it.

jokes.text: Dude. Def fuck him, 'Mad Men' style. Some real standard narrative bullshit. Crusty old dude, hot young girl, and then I'd Abbi-Jeremy him to even it out. Peg.
jokes.setup: They're playing subway fuck/marry/eat with fellow passengers.
jokes.punchline: Ilana identifies a 'Mad Men'-style office cliché scenario with a man, then says she'd 'Abbi-Jeremy him' — using her friend's sexual history as a verb meaning to peg someone — and then just says 'Peg.' deadpan.
jokes.explanation: 'Abbi-Jeremy him' as a verb is excellent — it takes a previous relationship detail from Abbi's life and turns it into a sexual verb, and doing so with such casual familiarity shows the depth of their friendship. The 'Some real standard narrative bullshit' cultural criticism is also very Ilana.

jokes.text: Bet she could teach me a thing or two, like, how to finish an article in the New Yorker. / I think I would marry her. / Wiccan wedding. / First dance to Wicked Wisdom. / Fist!
jokes.setup: Ilana is considering marrying a New Yorker-reading woman on the subway.
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 'I'd like to learn from her' to full Wiccan wedding with a 'Wicked Wisdom' first dance (Jada Pinkett Smith's metal band) to a fist bump as a toast is an absurdist spiral. The 'Fist!' is a complete non sequitur button.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Wicked Wisdom' (Jada Pinkett Smith's metal band — a real and deeply obscure choice) is very funny as a wedding playlist choice. The 'can't finish an article in the New Yorker' self-deprecation is relatable. The 'Fist!' button is the kind of Ilana non-sequitur that works when delivered with total commitment.

jokes.text: Okay, I would eat that baby. / Is that terrible of me? / No. / I just would. / No, it makes perfect sense.
jokes.setup: The fuck/marry/eat game continues; there's a baby in the subway car.
jokes.punchline: Ilana announces she'd eat the baby, then checks whether this is terrible. Abbi's 'No, it makes perfect sense' validates it with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: Ilana eating a baby is the darkest beat of the game, and Abbi's enablement ('no, it makes perfect sense') is what makes it work. The dynamic of Abbi validating Ilana's worst impulses is very character-specific. The reasoning that follows ('sparing her a life of tragedy and terror') is also very on-brand.

jokes.text: She looks delicious. / You would probably be sparing her a life of tragedy and terror, living down in the subway. / Plus, I feel like babies are, like, the most delicious in terms of actual meat.
jokes.setup: Ilana has decided to eat the baby.
jokes.punchline: The cannibal logic is applied seriously — Abbi spares the baby from mole-people life as a rationalization, then Ilana applies a food-quality framework to infant meat ('the most delicious in terms of actual meat').
jokes.explanation: The cannibal conversation escalates well. The callback to the mole people as a rationalization is structurally satisfying. 'The most delicious in terms of actual meat' is a horrifying sentence delivered as a food-quality assessment that lands from pure commitment.

jokes.text: Desperate times. / Calling for desperate measures. / And who are we to judge our post-apocalyptic selves? / What part would you eat first? / Which part is the most tender? / Probably like, the side or the butt.
jokes.setup: The cannibal baby conversation continues to escalate.
jokes.punchline: Abbi asking 'Which part is the most tender?' and the answer being 'the side or the butt' — delivered as if this is a reasonable butcher's question — is the escalation peak.
jokes.explanation: The 'Desperate times / Calling for desperate measures' is functional finish-the-idiom comedy. The real joke is the butchery specificity that follows — 'the side or the butt' applied to an infant. The straight-faced cannibal consultation between friends is sustained Broad City absurdism.

jokes.text: Dear God. Please make this subway move. / Ilana, that's not how it works. You can't just rub my butt, and the train...
jokes.setup: Ilana decides to pray for the subway to move.
jokes.punchline: Her prayer method is rubbing Abbi's butt (continuing from the 'butt is the most tender' conversation), which Abbi explains does not constitute prayer. The non-sequitur physical gesture as prayer is the joke.
jokes.explanation: This is a perfect callback — the butt conversation leads directly to Ilana physically touching Abbi's butt as a supernatural intervention. 'You can't just rub my butt, and the train...' followed by the train immediately moving is excellent comedic structure. The logical impossibility becoming real is very Broad City magic realism.

jokes.text: Ow! / Did it again, the power of the butt, bitch.
jokes.setup: Ilana rubbed Abbi's butt and the train started moving.
jokes.punchline: The train moving is attributed entirely to the butt-rubbing, and Ilana takes full supernatural credit: 'the power of the butt, bitch.'
jokes.explanation: 'The power of the butt, bitch' is the most quotable line in the episode. It has the cadence of a catchphrase and the absurdist logic of the whole sequence behind it. The 'Did it again' implies this is also a recurring phenomenon — giving Ilana a supernatural butt-power that has worked before. Near-perfect economy.

jokes.text: I'm really happy we didn't have to eat the baby. / Yeah. Happy.
jokes.setup: The subway starts moving, ending the crisis.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's genuine relief about not eating the baby — and Abbi's flat 'Yeah. Happy.' — is the quiet button on the extended sequence.
jokes.explanation: Perfect button on a long comedic sequence. The understatement of 'Yeah. Happy.' from Abbi with presumably flat delivery is ideal — she's as relieved as Ilana but won't dignify the conversation with enthusiasm. Clean, economical, structurally satisfying.

jokes.text: Uber surge fare is 12.5. / They really reel you in, and then they wring you fucking dry with their grubby little paws right around your neck. Then they make you type in the surge fare again, it's like, I know... I know what it is.
jokes.setup: They're stranded in Queens and need an Uber to get back.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's Uber rant escalates from valid complaint to cartoon villainy ('grubby little paws right around your neck') and then lands on the specific grievance of having to type in the surge price — a very specific, relatable indignity.
jokes.explanation: The Uber surge rant is solid observational comedy grounded in a genuine experience. The escalation from 'reel you in' to 'grubby little paws' is hyperbolic in a characteristically Ilana way. The landing on the typing-in-the-price grievance is the most specific and funniest part.

jokes.text: $1,200 from here to Gowanus back to JFK.
jokes.setup: They're looking at the Uber estimate to get back.
jokes.punchline: $1,200 delivered flat — the number itself is the joke. The gulf between what an Uber costs and what this would cost during surge pricing from outer Queens is the punchline.
jokes.explanation: The number alone does the work — the gap between expectation and reality is the entire joke. Excellent economy, but not especially crafted beyond the specific dollar amount.

jokes.text: I hate them. I love them, but I hate them.
jokes.setup: They've discovered the $1,200 Uber estimate.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's ambivalent 'I hate them. I love them, but I hate them' is the exact psychological truth of Uber dependency — you can't live with them, can't live without them.
jokes.explanation: Clean, relatable button. The self-contradiction ('I hate them / I love them, but I hate them') is very true to millennial Uber psychology and has a nice confessional quality. Not especially original but well-delivered.

jokes.text: Yeah. We hotwire that cab, drive ourselves within walking distance to JFK, soak it in gasoline, and torch it so they can't trace it... Let's go.
jokes.setup: Abbi spots an off-duty taxi; Ilana presents an 'escape plan.'
jokes.punchline: Ilana's plan escalates from 'hotwire a cab' to 'torch it to avoid forensics' — a full crime thriller arc for what is ultimately just borrowing a taxi — delivered as a completely reasonable solution.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from hotwiring to arson-for-forensic-evidence-destruction is great Ilana logic — she skips five steps of escalation as if they're obvious, and the 'Let's go' makes it sound like she has 100% confidence in this plan. Very character-specific.

jokes.text: I was gonna say maybe we could just knock on the door and see if they could drive us. / Oh, let's try that first.
jokes.setup: Ilana has proposed hotwiring and torching a cab.
jokes.punchline: Abbi suggests the obvious alternative — just knock — and Ilana's 'Oh, let's try that first' accepts the de-escalation without any embarrassment, as if arson was equally valid and she's simply choosing the more efficient option.
jokes.explanation: 'Oh, let's try that first' is a brilliant button. Ilana treats knocking as a preliminary option that should logically precede arson — as if they're just trying a softer approach before escalating to crime. The word 'first' implies arson is still on the table. Very economical.

jokes.text: Hello, sir. I know that this is ridiculous, but we would like to hire you to drive us in your taxi. To a few destinations.
jokes.setup: They knock on the off-duty cab driver's window.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's extremely formal, self-aware 'I know that this is ridiculous' approach — acknowledging the absurdity while still doing it — is the joke. The 'to a few destinations' tacked on at the end as an afterthought implies the request is about to get more complicated.
jokes.explanation: The 'to a few destinations' detail is the sharpest part — it signals more chaos to come. The self-awareness ('I know this is ridiculous') is a mild joke but sets up the extended negotiation scene well.

jokes.text: I couldn't be more off-duty. / Work is the worst, right? I get that, but listen...
jokes.setup: The cab driver (Anhur) rebuffs them.
jokes.punchline: Abbi immediately pivots to solidarity — 'Work is the worst, right? I get that' — before launching into her pitch, turning his objection into a bonding opportunity to soften him up.
jokes.explanation: Good people-pleasing character work from Abbi. The 'Work is the worst, right?' solidarity pivot is very recognizable human behavior in a negotiation, which makes it cringe-funny. Functional rather than exceptional.

jokes.text: I'm in trouble with the wife, you can understand. / ...Plus, there are good schools around here. / We are begging you.
jokes.setup: They're trying to convince Anhur to drive them.
jokes.punchline: Their pitch devolves into: Abbi inventing a fictional wife and marital trouble, Ilana complimenting the neighborhood's real estate and school districts — the mismatch of tactics (marital sympathy vs. neighborhood appreciation) is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The pitch going off the rails is a sustained cringe comedy beat. Ilana's school districts non-sequitur ('plus, there are good schools around here') applied to an Outer Queens street in this context is a funny absurdist pivot — real estate appreciation as a persuasion technique for a cab ride.

jokes.text: This beautiful neighborhood, it's gorgeous out here. — Quaint. — Lovely, I might look at some... — So sweet. — To buy or some... — I don't know, rent, whatever. — Plus, there are good schools around here.
jokes.setup: They're in a random Queens neighborhood trying to flatter the driver
jokes.punchline: The two women improvise increasingly absurd real estate enthusiasm for a random Queens street, with Abbi fully joining Ilana's bit and adding 'good schools' as the clincher
jokes.explanation: The two women's comedic partnership at its improvisational best — building a fiction together in real time. 'Good schools' as the ultimate suburban signifier is the right cap. Shows Abbi and Ilana's comedic lockstep.

jokes.text: How about... A crisp $100 bill from my bra?
jokes.setup: The negotiation has stalled.
jokes.punchline: Ilana produces a $100 bill from her bra — the 'crisp' qualifier and the bra location being equally funny. The formality of 'crisp' combined with the bra retrieval is perfect.
jokes.explanation: The word 'crisp' does all the work — it implies Ilana has been saving a pristine bill in her bra specifically for moments like this. It's a completely Ilana move: chaotic but somehow financially prepared in a very specific and unconventional way.

jokes.text: 'Fast and Furious' over here. / How old are you? / 15.
jokes.setup: Anhur turns out to be a terrible/reckless driver.
jokes.punchline: They ask how old he is; 'fifteen' — an illegal teen driver operating a taxi — is the absurdist escalation of their already chaotic situation.
jokes.explanation: Clean setup/punchline. The 'Fast and Furious' observation sets up the inquiry, and '15' is the perfect answer — specific, escalatory, completely wrong for the situation. The brevity is ideal.

jokes.text: Hold on. An old lady is pushing a baby stroller full of empty cans across the street. / Waiting. / She's almost across. / Okay, now we're coming around the corner.
jokes.setup: Abbi is talking Ilana through the cab's approach to Ilana's apartment so Ilana can run out and grab the passport.
jokes.punchline: The precision sportscasting is interrupted by a very specific and mundane NYC tableau — an old lady with a can-stroller — treated with full tactical seriousness.
jokes.explanation: The military-operation seriousness applied to picking up a passport while casually accommodating an old lady's can stroller is good absurdist/observational NYC comedy. The 'Waiting. She's almost across.' patience in the middle of an emergency is funny pacing.

jokes.text: Hey, slow down, but don't stop. / I gotcha, girl. / Hey! / Hi, honey. / Here's your passport, bye! / Thank you. / Yes! To the airport! / Love you, Himmelz! / Bye-bye, baby!
jokes.setup: They need to do a moving passport handoff without stopping.
jokes.punchline: The passport gets grabbed from a moving cab — a cinematic action sequence for a completely mundane task, played with full heist-movie energy including 'Love you, Himmelz!' as the getaway line.
jokes.explanation: The action-movie energy applied to passport retrieval is funny structurally. 'Love you, Himmelz!' as the getaway exclamation is a nice character beat — even in a crisis, Ilana's warmth is extravagant and specific. 'Bye-bye, baby!' from someone else makes it feel like a proper escape.

jokes.text: Ooh, whoops! / Oh, my brakes! Ay, my brakes! / Ay! Hold me closer, closer, closer. / Yes, ay!
jokes.setup: Anhur is the 15-year-old 'Fast and Furious' driver.
jokes.punchline: His brake failure (real or theatrical) during the getaway, with 'Hold me closer, closer, closer' — invoking either the Elton John song or just genuine panic — and then 'Yes, ay!' as if he's enjoying it.
jokes.explanation: Consistent with Anhur's established 15-year-old driver character. The brake panic played theatrically is more physical comedy than verbal. Functional but not highly crafted.

jokes.text: You only get so many important moments in your life, and you have to grasp them. Will you go to prom with me? Both of you? Or one of you? I don't care... I think I love you both. Can you be in love with two people?
jokes.setup: They thank Anhur for the ride; he gets emotional.
jokes.punchline: The 15-year-old taxi driver professes love for both of them and asks one or both to prom — treating the ride as a romantic peak experience.
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the 15-year-old driver reveal. His prom proposal is both adorable and completely inappropriate, and his earnestness — 'I think I love you both. Can you be in love with two people?' — is genuinely charming while being absurd. Good extended character comedy.

jokes.text: Yes, you definitely can. That is one of the most dangerous misconceptions about monogamy, but we can't go with you because you're a literal child.
jokes.setup: Anhur asks if you can be in love with two people.
jokes.punchline: Ilana answers his philosophical question completely seriously — 'one of the most dangerous misconceptions about monogamy' — before pivoting to the practical objection: he's a literal child.
jokes.explanation: This is excellent Ilana character comedy. She takes his philosophical question completely seriously, delivers a genuine opinion about monogamy ('one of the most dangerous misconceptions'), and only then notes the actual disqualification. The priority ordering — answer the philosophical question first, then note the age problem — is perfect character logic. 'Literal child' is also the right word choice.

jokes.text: Yeah, and don't worry, you're gonna have sex one day. / I've had sex. / Look at all my fucking bracelets.
jokes.setup: Abbi tries to console Anhur with reassurance about future sex.
jokes.punchline: Anhur immediately refutes this — 'I've had sex' — and then his proof is 'look at all my fucking bracelets.' The bracelets as evidence of sexual experience is a completely baffling logic that is instantly quotable.
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode and one of the most quotable sitcom lines of the season. 'Look at all my fucking bracelets' works on multiple levels: it's completely inscrutable as logic (what do bracelets have to do with having had sex?), it's delivered with total teenage conviction, and it uses the specificity of bracelets — a very teenage accessory — as evidence. The follow-up 'What the fuck are fucking bracelets?' from the women lands perfectly because the audience is also asking that question. Pure comedic originality — this line could not have been predicted and cannot be forgotten.

jokes.text: What the fuck are fucking bracelets? / Kids are terrifying.
jokes.setup: Anhur has cited his bracelets as proof of sexual experience.
jokes.punchline: 'What the fuck are fucking bracelets?' perfectly voices audience confusion, and 'Kids are terrifying' is the resigned conclusion — a perfect two-beat button.
jokes.explanation: The reaction doubles down on the bracelet joke brilliantly. 'What the fuck are fucking bracelets?' is the right response — baffled and linguistic — and 'Kids are terrifying' is the perfect conclusion, treating this inscrutable teenage logic as genuinely frightening. The economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Your period stain. / No, dude, it's an old stain. / It's a red herring, 'cause it's red and looks like a fish, kinda. / I'm packing. I'm packing heat. / I have weed in my vaginé. / Period pants are the ultimate decoy!
jokes.setup: At the airport security line, Abbi notices Ilana has a period stain on her pants.
jokes.punchline: Ilana reveals the stain is intentional — she's using period pants as a decoy for the weed she's carrying in her 'vaginé.' The 'red herring, cause it's red' pun and the 'vaginé' pronunciation are the cherry on top.
jokes.explanation: This scores 8+ because it's a genuinely original concept — using menstruation as a smuggling cover — delivered with complete Ilana conviction, including the terrible/perfect 'red herring' pun and the elevated 'vaginé' pronunciation. It's both raunchy and smart, and utterly character-specific. Only Ilana would have this plan and be proud of it.

jokes.text: Whoa! This dog is sexually harassing me!
jokes.setup: A sniffer dog approaches Ilana at airport security.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's immediate response to the drug-sniffing dog is to frame it as sexual harassment — perhaps because she has weed hidden in her 'vaginé' and the dog is sniffing at her crotch.
jokes.explanation: This scores 8+ because it's a perfect character-specific reaction. Of course Ilana frames a drug dog's crotch-sniffing as sexual harassment — it's the most Ilana possible interpretation of what's happening. The line is highly quotable and works both as a standalone line and as a callback to the weed-in-vaginé setup. Her reframing of drug detection as a personal affront is also funny on its own terms.

jokes.text: God! My period pants worked! / Dude, we should Shark Tank period pants, for real!
jokes.setup: The dog handler has pulled the dog away, embarrassed.
jokes.punchline: Ilana correctly identifies that her period-stain decoy confused the dog (or embarrassed the handler), then immediately pivots to entrepreneurial opportunity.
jokes.explanation: The Shark Tank pivot is great Ilana entrepreneurship — she immediately monetizes a drug-smuggling loophole as a business idea. The juxtaposition of 'my illegal weed survived airport security' → 'we should pitch this on TV' is absurdist and very character-true.

jokes.text: What the fuck! / Leave it! I'll leave mine! / No, dude, it's our whole luggage! / There, look! / Hey! / Not today, put it down! / Oh, I was just trying to take it to the lost and... / Yeah, sure you were! / Sure! / Piece of shit!
jokes.setup: Someone at the airport is apparently taking their luggage.
jokes.punchline: The immediate aggressive response ('Not today, put it down!') followed by the thief's transparent lost-and-found excuse being met with unified disbelief ('Yeah, sure you were! / Sure!') is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Good energy as a physical scene but less crafted in the dialogue. The synchronized 'Sure!' is a good beat, and the luggage thief's flimsy excuse is classic situational comedy. Functional airport chaos rather than a standout written joke.

jokes.text: What a sexy stare you have. / It's scary, too.
jokes.setup: Ilana runs past airport security, apparently making eye contact with an officer.
jokes.punchline: She compliments their stare with 'what a sexy stare you have' while sprinting past — and then adding 'it's scary too' as a qualifier — treating an intimidating authority figure as a flirtation opportunity while fleeing.
jokes.explanation: Purely Ilana to flirt with a security guard while running away from them with drugs hidden in her body. The 'it's scary, too' addendum is a perfect secondary beat — she's being accurate. The multi-tasking audacity is very character-specific.

jokes.text: You should have taken the walkway. / So stubborn.
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana are sprinting past the moving walkway.
jokes.punchline: A bystander critiques their sprint choice mid-emergency with complete composure: 'So stubborn.' The audacity of the unsolicited airport advice during their crisis is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan from a minor character. 'So stubborn' as the follow-up to 'you should have taken the walkway' is funny because it's a complete diagnosis of a stranger's behavior from a two-second observation. The economy is very strong.

jokes.text: Hey, you grabbed our boards, bro!
jokes.setup: They're sprinting through the airport.
jokes.punchline: Ilana, while running to catch a flight, apparently grabs someone's skateboard or surfboard (inferred from 'boards') and is called out by the owner.
jokes.explanation: Mostly a physical visual gag (boards at an airport, the sprint requisitioning random equipment). Less funny as pure text but the chaos energy is appropriate for the sequence.

jokes.text: Ilana! / I got an extra ticket, let's do this! / Do what, Dale? / What the fuck is wrong with you?
jokes.setup: Someone named Dale apparently offers Ilana a ticket for something in the middle of the airport sprint.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's complete non-sequitur 'Do what, Dale?' and Abbi's disgusted 'What the fuck is wrong with you?' implies Dale is making a wildly inappropriate or out-of-context proposition during the crisis.
jokes.explanation: The 'Do what, Dale?' is a funny beat — someone is offering something completely out of context in the middle of a sprint. The named character 'Dale' adds specificity. Abbi's moral outrage response is the button. Partly visual comedy.

jokes.text: Oh! Sorry! / You fucking bitch! / I didn't mean to hit you.
jokes.setup: They reach the gate; Ilana apparently hits the flight attendant.
jokes.punchline: The flight attendant's 'You fucking bitch!' — a flight attendant saying this, professionally — is the shock joke. Ilana's 'I didn't mean to hit you' is the mild, sincere follow-up.
jokes.explanation: The flight attendant's outburst is the joke — the inversion of expected professional behavior. Functional physical comedy with a good subversive edge.

jokes.text: I was just trying to get one foot in the door. / It's a shoe, not a foot, and it hit me, not a door.
jokes.setup: Ilana uses the idiom 'get one foot in the door' to explain why she ran at the flight attendant.
jokes.punchline: The flight attendant corrects both literal elements of the idiom: it's a shoe (not a foot) and it hit her (not a door).
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay structure. The joke methodically deconstructs the idiom by correcting its two literal inaccuracies ('shoe, not a foot / hit me, not a door') in a completely deadpan way. The flight attendant treating Ilana's metaphor as a statement of fact is a perfect straight-man move. Very economical and structurally clean.

jokes.text: We all hate her. / Thank you. / You are two lucky Jews. / We think so, too.
jokes.setup: The flight attendant grudgingly lets them board.
jokes.punchline: The flight attendant's audible 'We all hate her' is a complete violation of professional affect. Then 'You are two lucky Jews' — naming their Jewishness in context of their luck getting on this Israel-bound flight — and Ilana's completely accepting 'We think so, too.'
jokes.explanation: 'We all hate her' said within earshot is excellent subversive flight attendant behavior. 'You are two lucky Jews' on a Birthright flight is character-specific and lands because the show owns its Jewishness completely — and 'We think so, too' is the perfect breezy acceptance.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, we fucking made it! / Oh, God, I cannot believe we made it. / This is the longest ramp I've ever been on.
jokes.setup: They've made the flight and are celebrating.
jokes.punchline: The celebration is immediately deflated by the practical complaint about the jetway being really long — going from triumphant to mundanely annoyed in seconds.
jokes.explanation: Good tonal deflation. The longest ramp complaint is petty and perfect — they just survived a complete crisis and their first complaint is the jetway length. Very character-specific in its immediate return to mundane grievance.

jokes.text: I am so sorry, ladies. There is no more space in the overhead compartments, we'll have to check your luggage. / Actually, yours will probably be okay. So sleek and streamlined. / Lincoln... had really adult taste in luggage.
jokes.setup: They finally board the plane. Their luggage needs to be checked.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's luggage (Lincoln's) is called 'sleek and streamlined' by the flight attendant, triggering Abbi's emotional reference to Lincoln — the ex-boyfriend breakup grief resurfaces through luggage quality.
jokes.explanation: A strong callback. The sleek Lincoln luggage compliment triggers breakup grief expressed through the sentence 'Lincoln had really adult taste in luggage' — a funny way to mourn someone. The word 'adult' does work here, implying Lincoln was more grown-up than Abbi, which adds a layer.

jokes.text: Lincoln... had really adult taste in luggage. / This is actually a really special bag, too. It's Drew's. / Sorry, this is not your bag? / No, no, no, it's mine... I packed it. It's just that Ms. Drew Barrymore highly recommends this bag.
jokes.setup: The flight attendant questions Abbi's ownership of her Drew Barrymore bag.
jokes.punchline: Abbi defending her ownership by saying she packed it — but also calling Drew Barrymore 'Ms. Drew Barrymore' with formal reverence — is funny. 'Lincoln had really adult taste in luggage' is a good character note landing quietly in a crisis.
jokes.explanation: The 'Lincoln had really adult taste' callback adds emotional texture while being funny. 'Ms. Drew Barrymore' as Abbi's formal reference to the celebrity is a nice character moment — she genuinely respects Drew.

jokes.text: This bag is the bomb. Please just let me... I'm sure there's a spot. / Okay, I'm gonna shut these doors and take off. / Would you like to come with us? / Mark, can you come get this bag, please?
jokes.setup: Abbi protests having to check the Drew Barrymore suitcase.
jokes.punchline: 'This bag is the bomb' — Abbi says this on a plane, which the flight attendant treats as a threat or at least absurdity. The flight attendant's response ('Would you like to come with us?') is the driest possible threat/invitation before summoning 'Mark' — the luggage handler.
jokes.explanation: 'This bag is the bomb' on a plane has the potential to be played much bigger, but the show wisely underplays it. The flight attendant's 'Would you like to come with us?' is the right deadpan response — ambiguously threatening. Good cringe setup for the Drew Barrymore payoff.

jokes.text: Do you think it's gonna be okay? / I'm sure your bag is in really... good hands. / Thanks so much. / Whoa! Nice! / International travel is amazing.
jokes.setup: Abbi's precious Drew Barrymore bag is taken away by 'Mark' the luggage handler.
jokes.punchline: The flight attendant's pause before 'good hands' implies they've witnessed what just happened to the bag — presumably 'Mark' throwing it or mishandling it. The 'Whoa! Nice!' confirms some luggage brutality has occurred. 'International travel is amazing' is ironic.
jokes.explanation: The 'really... good hands' pause is a great piece of inferred visual comedy — the audience understands the bag is being destroyed even without seeing it. 'Whoa! Nice!' confirms it. The callback to the Drew Barrymore bag gives this emotional stakes for Abbi that make the joke land harder.

jokes.text: It's just that Ms. Drew Barrymore highly recommends this bag. If you Google, in quotes, 'Refinery 29,' in quotes, 'Drew Barrymore.' That's amazing. Unfortunately, Drew Barrymore is the only person who could carry on something that bulky.
jokes.setup: Abbi's suitcase needs to be checked
jokes.punchline: Abbi cites Drew Barrymore as luggage authority ('Ms. Drew Barrymore'); the flight attendant acknowledges this is amazing but says Drew Barrymore alone can carry it on — implying the rules apply differently for Drew
jokes.explanation: The callback delivers a new joke: Drew Barrymore apparently has carry-on privileges nobody else has. 'Ms. Drew Barrymore' as an honorific is perfect Abbi reverence. 'That's amazing. Unfortunately...' is a great deadpan beat from the flight attendant.

jokes.text: No, this is first class. / We gotta keep going. / Ms. Fessiwig, champagne or orange juice? / Orange juice it is. / Oh, my God. / I know, who chooses juice over champagne? / Ew, coach.
jokes.setup: They briefly wander into first class.
jokes.punchline: The first-class passenger choosing orange juice over champagne is treated as a profound moral failure by Ilana ('who chooses juice over champagne?'). Then the dismissive 'Ew, coach' on entering their actual section is the perfect Ilana reversal — she would never.
jokes.explanation: 'Who chooses juice over champagne?' voiced with genuine outrage, and then 'Ew, coach' delivered with the disdain Ilana reserves for things she cannot afford — a very specific class-aspirational Ilana reaction. The irony is she's in coach.

jokes.text: Okay, I'm gonna shut these doors and take off. Would you like to come with us?
jokes.setup: Abbi is mid-debate about her bag.
jokes.punchline: The flight attendant's casual 'would you like to come with us?' — delivered as if leaving Abbi behind is genuinely an option she might not take — is perfectly deadpan.
jokes.explanation: The flight attendant's power play is gentle but effective. 'Would you like to come with us?' framed as if it's optional is the right comedic choice — understated rather than aggressive.

jokes.text: Hello. Um... / Ilana. / Hi. / And... / Abbi. / Yes. / I'm Jared. Welcome to Birthmarc. / Surf's up.
jokes.setup: They reach their seats and are greeted by their Birthright trip leader.
jokes.punchline: 'Welcome to Birthmarc' — not 'Birthright' — is the first signal of Jared's unhinged energy. Then 'Surf's up' as a greeting to plane passengers going to Israel.
jokes.explanation: 'Birthmarc' instead of 'Birthright' establishes Jared as a specific kind of zealot who has named his program after himself or a version thereof. 'Surf's up' to a plane full of people going to Israel is perfectly absurd. Good character introduction for Jared.

jokes.text: Ms. Fessiwig, champagne or orange juice? / Orange juice it is. / Oh, my God. / I know, who chooses juice over champagne? Ew, coach.
jokes.setup: In first class, a flight attendant offers a passenger champagne or OJ.
jokes.punchline: A passenger chooses orange juice over champagne. Ilana's 'who chooses juice over champagne? Ew, coach.' — 'coach' deployed as an insult for the mindset rather than the seat — is a quick sharp joke.
jokes.explanation: 'Ew, coach' as an adjective/insult for the wrong mindset is very quotable. Coming from someone walking through first class on their way to coach makes Ilana's snobbishness particularly rich.

jokes.text: For the next ten days, we will be traveling around the holiest place in the world, Israel. For free! / Gotcha, Jews.
jokes.setup: Jared introduces the Birthright trip.
jokes.punchline: 'Gotcha, Jews' — Jared punctuates the Birthright pitch by essentially acknowledging it as a cultural/religious recruiting trap. The casual antisemitic-adjacent framing deployed by an apparent Jewish trip leader is the transgressive joke.
jokes.explanation: 'Gotcha, Jews' is a genuinely daring line that works because it's self-directed — a Jewish person (presumably) acknowledging the Birthright program as a recruitment mechanism with complete candor. The economy is near-perfect: two words do enormous work. It's the kind of line that lands on rewatches harder than first viewing.

jokes.text: our beautiful country will teach you all about Judaism, its rich history, and I'm looking at the two of you, its reproductive future.
jokes.setup: Jared continues his Birthright pitch.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm looking at the two of you, its reproductive future' — singling out Abbi and Ilana as specifically important for Jewish reproduction, delivered with complete seriousness.
jokes.explanation: The reproductive future framing — delivered while staring at two specific women — is a great cringe comedy beat. It exposes the underlying demographic anxiety of Birthright in the most uncomfortable and specific way possible. Jared's complete seriousness makes it funnier.

jokes.text: For the next ten days, we will be traveling around the holiest place in the world, Israel. For free! Gotcha, Jews.
jokes.setup: Jared pitches the Birthright trip to Ilana and Abbi.
jokes.punchline: 'Gotcha, Jews' — Jared's gleeful acknowledgment that the free trip is the lure that works on Jewish people — is simultaneously accurate, slightly uncomfortable, and very funny.
jokes.explanation: 'Gotcha, Jews' lands because it's honest, slightly self-mocking for Jewish audience members, and surprising coming from the Jared character. The economy is exceptional — two words do a lot. The dark edge (using stereotypes as self-aware commentary) is the kind of joke Broad City earns because of who's writing it.
